coredata.dc:title: Preparation of a novel polysulfone membrane by incorporated with carbon dots grafted silica from rice husk for dye removal
coredata.dc:creator: Heng, Zeng Wei
Tan, Yee Yen
Chong, Woon Chan
Mahmoudi, Ebrahim
Mohammad, Abdul Wahab
Teoh, Hui Chieh
Sim, Lan Ching
Koo, Chai Hoon
coredata.dc:description: This study demonstrated the modification of polysulfone (PSf) membrane by incorporating carbon dots (CDs) and silica (SiO2) as additives for membrane’s permeability and dye rejection enhancement. Both the CDs and SiO2 were synthesized from rice husk and the carbon dots were impregnated on SiO2 to form CD/SiO2 composite through a hydrothermal synthesis reaction. The impregnation of CDs had shown increment of hydrophilic functional groups with negative surface charge on the SiO2. A series of CD/SiO2 composite embedded ultrafiltration (UF) membranes was developed. Results revealed that the CD/SiO2 additive was endowed with enhanced membrane properties in terms of pore size, pore distribution, hydrophilicity and negative surface charge. All the modified membranes demonstrated higher permeability and dye rejection. Among the membranes, 1.0 wt% of CD/SiO2 loading showed excellent pure water permeability increment of 111 % due to improved membrane hydrophilicity and enlargement of membrane pores. Notably, the 2.5 wt% of CD/SiO2 membrane showed the highest dye removal of 50.54 % compared to pure PSf membrane of 14.52 % albeit it had similar permeability as the pure PSf membrane. This study had demonstrated a circular economy approach in turning rice husk biomass into useful additives for membrane nanotechnology application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Silica
Polymeric membrane
Hydrophilicity
Dye removal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of silica from rice husk
Synthesis of carbon dot solution from rice husk
Fabrication of membranes incorporated with additives
Morphology study of SiO2 and CD/SiO2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to acknowledge Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman (IPSR/RMC/UTARRF/2019-C1/C07) for the grants provided. Besides, the support from our research centre, CPAMR is greatly appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of CD, SiO2 and CD/SiO2 composite.
XRD patterns for SiO2 and CD/SiO2.
SEM images of (a) SiO2 and (b) CD/SiO2 powders.

SEM cross-sectional images for the fabricated membranes at the magnification of × 1.5k: (a) pure PSf (b) 0.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loading (c) 1.0 wt% CD/SiO2 loading (d) 1.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loading (e) 2.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loading.

SEM top view images for the fabricated membranes at the magnification of × 6.0k: (a) pure PSf (b) 0.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loading (c) 1.0 wt% CD/SiO2 loading (d) 1.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loading (e) 2.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loading.
EDX mapping of the element (a) Si, (b) O, (c) C and (d) S present on the CD/SiO2 incorporated membrane.
Permeability of membranes with different CD/SiO2 loading.
Dye rejection of membranes at different CD/SiO2 loading.
Composition of the membrane casting solutions.
Porosity and pore size of membranes.

head.ce:title: Preparation of a novel polysulfone membrane by incorporated with carbon dots grafted silica from rice husk for dye removal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Sustainable Process Technology (CESPRO)
Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Sustainable Process Technology (CESPRO), Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study demonstrated the modification of polysulfone (PSf) membrane by incorporating carbon dots (CDs) and silica (SiO2) as additives for membrane’s permeability and dye rejection enhancement. Both the CDs and SiO2 were synthesized from rice husk and the carbon dots were impregnated on SiO2 to form CD/SiO2 composite through a hydrothermal synthesis reaction. The impregnation of CDs had shown increment of hydrophilic functional groups with negative surface charge on the SiO2. A series of CD/SiO2 composite embedded ultrafiltration (UF) membranes was developed. Results revealed that the CD/SiO2 additive was endowed with enhanced membrane properties in terms of pore size, pore distribution, hydrophilicity and negative surface charge. All the modified membranes demonstrated higher permeability and dye rejection. Among the membranes, 1.0 wt% of CD/SiO2 loading showed excellent pure water permeability increment of 111 % due to improved membrane hydrophilicity and enlargement of membrane pores. Notably, the 2.5 wt% of CD/SiO2 membrane showed the highest dye removal of 50.54 % compared to pure PSf membrane of 14.52 % albeit it had similar permeability as the pure PSf membrane. This study had demonstrated a circular economy approach in turning rice husk biomass into useful additives for membrane nanotechnology application.

ce:section.ce:para: The raging global water pollution issue due to the excessive discharge of industrial effluent into fresh water has highlighted the irrefutable importance of wastewater treatment in overcoming the scarcity of clean water supply. As water is the building block of life, the water contamination issues are gaining world wide’s attention and urge the reuse of water. Membrane separation technology as an advanced treatment method is commonly integrated into the wastewater treatment system to ensure the top-notch quality of the treated water [1].

The discharge of dye-containing effluent into the environment from food and textile industries have contributed significantly to the water pollution. For a worldwide dye production of approximately 800,000 tons/year, about 10–15% of the synthetic dyes are discharged as waste without processing [2]. Dye discharge has brought upon more than 20 % of the water pollution in countries such as Turkey, Indonesia and China [3]. Membrane filtration which is also known as the polishing step after the secondary treatment could be used in treating dye-containing wastewater [1]. Though, the cost of a membrane filtration system is higher compared with the conventional treatment process. Recently, the development of nanotechnology has created a new generation of membrane − nanocomposite membrane, to lower the energy consumption and operational cost of the system. In addition, previous study by Chong et al. (2018) [4] revealed that the overall environmental footprint of the application of nanomaterials in the membrane filtration system outweighed its fabrication life cycle due to its excellent filtration performance. The research pointed out that the application of nanomaterials in membrane technology is more sustainable in a long-term operation.

Carbon based nanomaterials such as graphene and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) had shown excellent performance improvement in membrane modification. Chung et al. (2017) [5] fabricated UF membrane embedded with graphene oxide (GO) composite. The composite membrane demonstrated high permeability, excellent rejection and great anti-fouling ability. Later, Chong et al. (2019) [6] benchmarked the self-fabricated Ag/GO (silver grafted on GO) membrane with a commercial membrane in an algal membrane photoreactor. The study showed that the nanocomposite membrane not only exhibited greater fouling propensity, but also enhanced anti-microbial ability. Intrchom et al. (2019) [7] modified PTFE membrane with carboxylated CNTs for ammonia separation. An increment of 63 % of flux was observed. Furthermore, the ammonia selectivity of the modified membrane did not deteriorate with temperature, as compared with the PTFE membrane. Although these nanomaterials show good improvement of membrane performance, tedious production process and higher cost inhibited its production in a large scale [8].

Carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanoparticle with the size of 10 nm or lesser, with abundance of oxygen functional groups had shown excellent potential in various research areas such as photoelectric device, biomedical, polymer and photocatalysis [9,10]. CDs could be synthesized from multifarious methods with a wide variety of raw materials including polymer, natural resources, graphite, food and small molecules [11]. Among these materials, the production of CDs from biomass as raw material is green and environmentally friendly.

Previous studies have shown that CDs had high hydrophilicity, tunable surface functional groups and favorable polymer affinity [12]. Hence, it is suitable to be used for membrane modification. To date, a few studies explored the application of CDs in membrane fabrication. However, they were mainly focus on utilization of CDs in thin film membrane fabrication [13–15]. Sun et al. [16] functionalized CDs with carboxyl, amino and sulfonic acid groups prior incorporated into polyamide layer of the thin film composite. All the modified membranes demonstrated higher permeability and greater anti-fouling ability compared with the unmodified membrane. Later, Gai et al. [17] modified a thin film composite with sodium ion (Na+) functionalized CDs. The membrane permeability increased 47.1 % at 15 bar without compromising its sodium chloride (NaCl) rejection. The improvement of the modified membranes was owing to increment of oxygen functioning groups, effective surface area and interstitial space between the polyamide chains.

The main objective of this study was to fabricate novel carbon dots-silica (CD/SiO2) mixed matrix membrane (MMM) with high permeability and rejection via phase inversion method. Both CDs and SiO2 were synthesized from rice husk which is an agricultural biomass of rice production. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study of blending CD/SiO2 nanocomposite in the PSf polymer matrix to efficiently improve the overall performance of UF membrane. The abundance of oxygen functional groups on CDs surface could serve as anchoring points for coupling with SiO2 to produce CD/SiO2 composite as membrane additives. The role of the additives produced from biomass in enhancing the physical and chemical properties of the membrane was elucidated.

Rice husk was obtained from the rice field after the harvesting season. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4) and phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Caustic soda (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) were obtained from Merck. Polysulfone (PSf) polymer Udel P-3500 in pellet form was bought from Solvay. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as membrane pore forming agent was purchased from R&M. All the chemicals were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silica from rice husk
ce:section.ce:para: The rice husks collected was sieved and rinsed with distilled water to remove the impurities. Subsequently, they were dried to remove moisture content prior to the carbonization process. The dried rice husks were carbonized with 40 % H3PO4 with a volume to weight ratio of 2:1 in a chamber furnace at 450 °C to produce rice husk ash (RHA). The H3PO4 served as an oxidizing agent to form oxides of metallic ions [18]. Afterwards, the carbonized rice husks were washed with distilled water and dried in the hot air oven to produce black RHA powder. Later, 10 g of RHA produced was treated with 100 mL of 2 M HCl and stirred for 2 h. The acid treatment functioned to remove the metallic contents in the RHA. After filtered with a filter paper, the RHA residue was rinsed with distilled water to dissolve the remaining metallic salts prior drying with hot air oven [18].

Thereafter, 1 M NaOH was added into the RHA suspension and stirred in a water bath of 80 °C for 2 h. By reacting the SiO2 presented in the RHA with the alkaline NaOH, a carbon-free sodium silicate (Na2SiO3) solution was formed and the mixture was filtered using a filter paper. The carbon residues were washed with distilled water to collect the remnants of Na2SiO3 [19]. After that, 2 M HCl was titrated into the solution to produce SiO2 gel precipitate. The SiO2 gel was aged for 24 h at room temperature and distilled water was added to break the SiO2 gel into a slurry comprising solid gel of SiO2 and sodium chloride (NaCl) solution. Lastly, the slurry was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min to separate the solid SiO2 gel from the NaCl solution. Pure SiO2 xerogel was successfully obtained after washing and drying [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dot solution from rice husk
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize CDs, the collected rice husks were pre-treated and blended into fine particles using a countertop blender. Subsequently, the grounded rice husks were refluxed with 1 M H2SO4 at the ratio of 15 g of rice husks to 500 mL of H2SO4 for an hour at 80 °C with vigorous stirring. This was to functionalize the carbon group presented in the rice husks by forming oxides and hydroxides of carbon [20]. Then, the acid treated rice husks slurry was diluted with distilled water and vacuum filtered to obtain a yellowish-brown CD solution as filtrate.

To graft CDs onto SiO2, 1 g of SiO2 was ultrasonicated with 60 mL of CD solution to ensure that the SiO2 powder was mixed homogeneously with the CD nanoparticles. Then, the ultrasonicated mixture was hydrothermally treated in a 150 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 175 °C for 5 h to promote the carbon framework cutting of CDs, in which the amount of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups attached on the carbon framework was chemically reduced. At the same time, the hydrothermal reaction also allows the grafting of CDs onto SiO2 to occur simultaneously to form Si-O-C linkages [21].

Subsequently, the hydrothermally treated mixture was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The light brown supernatant was then discarded, and the brown precipitate remained in the centrifuge tubes was washed twice with distilled water. Lastly, the washed brown precipitate was dried in the hot air oven at 70 °C for 3 days to obtain CD/SiO2 composite as brown powder [20]. Fig. S1 shows the SiO2 in white color and turned into brown color after grafted with CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of membranes incorporated with additives
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate membranes using non-solvent induced phase inversion (NIPS) method, membrane casting solutions were prepared according to the composition as shown in Table 1 . Pure PSf membrane and MMMs with four different dosages of CD/SiO2 composite (0.5 wt%, 1.0 wt%, 1.5 wt% and 2.5 wt%) were prepared. The solution with additives were ultrasonicated followed by stirring to ensure a homogeneous mixing of the additives and the solvent. The casting solution was sealed with parafilm and stirred at 60 °C and 600 rpm for 4 h to ensure that polymer was dissolved. Subsequently, the solution was stirred overnight at 100 rpm at room temperature to ensure homogeneous mixing.

After degassing, the casting solution was spread uniformly on a clean, flat glass plate with the aid of a casting knife while maintaining a gap of approximately 0.2 mm between the casting knife and the glass plate. After that, the glass plate with the casted film was immersed immediately into a water bath at room temperature to allow the occurrence of the solvent and non-solvent exchange, which resulted in the formation of membranes [22].

The characterization of membrane additives is important as it gives an insight on the expected membrane characteristics. FTIR technique (IS10, Nicolet) was used to identify the functional groups and chemical bonds presented on the SiO2 and CD/SiO2 samples. The FTIR was run at the scanning range of 400 to 4000 cm−1 and the FTIR spectra of both samples were collected. The XRD technique (XRD-6000, Shidmazu) was used to investigate the crystallinity and structural characteristics of SiO2 and CD/SiO2. A typical Copper K-Alpha (CuK- α ) X-ray beam with a wavelength of 1.5406 Å was selected as the radiation source and the sample was scanned from 10 ° to 80 ° with a scan speed of 2 ° /min. SEM (S-3400 N, Hitachi) was employed to observe and compare the surface morphologies of the SiO2 and CD/SiO2 composite. The samples in powder form were sprinkled onto carbon adhesives and coated with a thin layer of gold-palladium (Au-Pd) using a sputter coater. Subsequently, the EDX analysis was carried out on the samples without the Au-Pd coatings to detect the elemental composition present on the nanoparticles. The surface charge of SiO2 and CD/SiO2 was discerned by the zeta potential using zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments).

SEM was used to observe the structure of the membranes in plan and cross-sectional views. The membranes were broken with liquid nitrogen for the cross-sectional view analysis. EDX technique was also employed to determine the elemental composition present on the membranes as well as to identify the distribution of the additives on the surface of the membranes [23].

To measure the porosity and pore size of the membranes, wet membranes were cut into tiny pieces of 1 cm × 1 cm and the water droplets on the membrane surfaces were dabbed with a filter paper. The membranes were then weighed (denoted as W1 ) and dried overnight in a hot air oven at 50 ℃ . After that, the dried membranes were weighed again and the value was denoted as W2 . Subsequently, the thickness of the membranes, l was measured using a dial indicator thickness gauge. The membrane porosity, ε was computed using the gravimetric method as shown in Eq. 1 [24]. Using the membrane porosity, the membrane mean pore radius was determined by the Guerout-Elford-Ferry equation as shown in Eq. (2).
(1)
ε =
W 1
-
W 2
A l
ρ w
(2)
r m
=
2.9 - 1.75 ε
8 η l Q
ε × A × Δ P
,
where,
ρ w
is the density of distilled water, η is the viscosity of water (Pa ∙ s), l is the thickness of membrane (m), Q is the volumetric flowrate of pure water permeate (m3/ s), A is the area of membrane (m2) and Δ P is the operational pressure (Pa).

The surface hydrophilicity of the membranes was determined by the water contact angle on the membrane surfaces [22]. The sessile drop technique was employed to measure the angle between the water droplet and the membrane surface with the aid of a contact angle goniometer (FM40Mk2, KRÜSS GmbH) armed with a high-resolution camera.

The pure water permeability, P of the membranes were determined using a dead-end filtration cell unit and then computed using Eq. 3.
(3)
P =
Q
A × Δ t × Δ P
,
where Q is the volume of permeate (L), A is the area of membrane (m2), Δ t is the time taken for the permeate collection (h) and Δ P is the transmembrane pressure difference (bar).

The rejection ability of the membranes was performed by filtering 100 mg/ L of tartrazine dye solution using the dead-end filtration cell system at 2 bar. The permeate was collected in a beaker for 60 min. and the absorbance of the permeate was measured using a uv–vis spectrophotometer at the wavelength of 427 nm. Prior to this, a calibration curve of absorbance against concentration of tartrazine was plotted [25]. The dye rejection, R of each membranes can be computed using Eq. 4.
(4)
R % =
1 -
C p
C f
× 100
% ,
where Cp and Cf is the concentration of tartrazine dye for permeate and feed in mg/L, respectively.

The functional groups present in the synthesized membrane additives were identified through the FTIR analysis. Fig. 1 depicts the FTIR spectra of CD, SiO2 and CD/SiO2 composite. Referring to the FTIR spectrum of CD, the peak at 1620 cm−1 was identified as the CO stretching vibration [26]. The prominent broad band observed at 3400 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the OH group [20]. The presence of the carbonyl (CO) and hydroxyl (OH) groups signified that CD was attained through the reflux of rice husks in H2SO4 [21].

As for the SiO2 sample, the peak observed at the wavenumber of 451 cm−1 was accounted as both in-plane bending vibration and out-of-plane rocking motion of Si − O–Si. The absorption peaks attained at 810 cm-1 and 1080 cm−1 was identified as symmetric and bending modes of Si − O–Si [19]. Meanwhile, the peak at 810 cm-1 and broad peak at 3410 cm−1 were the OH bending vibration [26]. In essence, the functional groups identified through the few characteristic peaks found in the FTIR spectrum of the SiO2 sample was equivalent to that of the chemical structure of SiO2.

On the other hand, several distinctive peaks were spotted in the FTIR spectrum of CD/SiO2 composite. Both peaks of 460 and 900 cm−1 were identified as Si-O-Si linkages as reported by Wang et al. (2017) [21]. The absorption peak detected at 1100 cm-1 was assigned as the Si−OH bending vibration, while the broad band perceived at 3500 cm-1 was attributed to the Si−OH stretching vibration. Besides, the characteristic peak at 1730 cm-1 was accounted to the peak of CO, which was contributed by CD [26]. Comparing the CO peak of CD/SiO2 to that of CD, the CO peak of CD/SiO2 was noticed to have shifted towards a larger wavenumber. This might be caused by the reduction in the mass of CO molecules, in which the double bond was broken to form linkage with the silica structure in SiO2 in the form of SiOC. As a result, the mass reduction of the CO molecules led to the increment of the vibration frequency and wavenumber [27]. Notably, the SiOC vibration located at the wavenumber of 1140 cm-1 indicates that covalent linkages were formed between the carbon framework of the CD and the silica structure of SiO2 during the grafting of CD onto SiO2 [21]. Hence, CD/SiO2 composite was successfully synthesized based on the chemical structure linkages identified in the FTIR spectrum.

The XRD analysis was conducted on the SiO2 and CD/SiO2 samples to determine their crystallinity and structural characteristics. Fig. 2 depicts the XRD patterns obtained for both samples. Referring to the XRD pattern of the SiO2 sample, a broad hump detected at 2 θ = 22.8 ° was assigned to amorphous SiO2 as reported by Rohaeti, et al. (2010) [28]. Five narrow and distinctive peaks located at 2 θ = 31.69, 45.43, 56.47, 66.36 and 75.28 ° were indexed as silicon (Si) with different reflection planes of (111), (220), (311), (400) and (331), respectively in the crystalline phase. The lattice structural pattern observed was similar to that of the SiO2 sample prepared in the work by [28]. However, the peaks were slightly shifted from their original position due to change in lattice parameters resulting from the lattice expansion or contraction during the ashing and leaching process with different treatment condition [29].

Similarly, according to the diffraction pattern attained for the CD/SiO2 sample, Si (111) and Si (220) were accounted at the peak positions of 2 θ of 37.90° and 44.16°, respectively. At the same time, amorphous SiO2 was detected around 2 θ of 18° to 20° [28]. Although similar compounds were assigned to both samples, the peak positions of these compounds were observed to differ between both samples. This might be caused by the small changes in the lattice structural parameter such as the lattice spacings eliciting from the incorporation of dopants into the sample [30]. Hence, the lattice structural parameter of the SiO2 sample was re-orientated by the grafting of CD onto SiO2, resulting in the shift of the Si peaks. In addition, the alter in the lattice parameter also caused the Si reflecting planes present in CD/SiO2 to differ from that of SiO2. Furthermore, a weak and broad peak detected around 22–29 ° was accounted to the amorphous CD nanoparticles, which is frequently superimposed with the peak position of highly disordered carbon atoms and the graphitic (002) state of carbon [31]. As the CD nanoparticles have relatively smaller particle size and greater interlayer spacing due to the abundance of functional groups present, the CD peak has lower peak intensity and relatively greater FWHM, which leads to peak broadening.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology study of SiO2 and CD/SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the surface morphologies of the additives, SEM and EDX analysis were conducted. The SEM images obtained for both additives at the magnification of × 4.20k are depicted in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3(a) shows the irregular spherical particles of SiO2. On the other hand, Fig. 3(b) displays the grafting of CD nanoparticles onto the SiO2 particles. Some of the SiO2 particles were observed to have engulfed by the CD nanoparticles and slight agglomeration of the grafted particles can be spotted. This might be due to the hygroscopic characteristic of the CD that causes the nanoparticles to agglutinate and turned sticky when exposed to the moisture in air [32]. The TEM image of the CD is presented in Fig. S2.

Subsequently, the elemental composition present in the SiO2 and CD/SiO2 powder was shown in the EDX spectra in Fig. S3 and summarized in Table S1. It was observed that the elements of Si (silica) and O (oxygen) were detected for the SiO2 sample, while Si, O, C (carbon) and S (sulphur) elements were recognized in the CD/SiO2 sample. The presence of the S element in the CD/ SiO2 composite was caused by the acid treatment of rice husks using H2SO4 to form CD. The traces of H2SO4 in the CD/SiO2 composite could not be easily removed by means of evaporation as it has a high boiling point. Comparing both samples, the ratio of Si to O elements accounted to CD/SiO2 was reduced as compared to that of SiO2. This further validates the grafting of SiO2 particles by the CD nanoparticles as discussed earlier in Section 3.1.3.

Fig. 4 presents the cross-sectional images of all membranes. Finger-like and tear-shaped structures were observed for all the membranes. This was contributed by the porous membrane formation through the instantaneous de-mixing of polymer and solvent at the casting solution-water interface during the phase inversion process [25].

The pure PSf membrane shows densely packed, short finger-like voids at the top sublayer and small spongy structures at the bottom sublayer. This was due to the absence of CD/SiO2 additives in the polymer solution. On the other hand, MMMs with 0.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loading exhibited loosely packed, longer and wider finger-like structures at the top sublayer and larger macrovoids at the bottom sublayer as compared to the pure PSf membrane. This was owing to the incorporation of the self-synthesized CD/SiO2 additives when fabricating the membranes. By introducing the hydrophilic CD/SiO2 additives into the membrane casting solution, the thermodynamic improved, leading to a higher exchange rate between the solvent and non-solvent. This resulted in enhanced kinetic hindrance that aided the inter-diffusion velocity and speed up the phase inversion rate [12]. Hence, porous membranes with larger and longer pores can be formed with the presence of additives in the membranes.

Referring to Fig. 4(c), when the catalyst loading increased to 1 wt%, the macrovoids at the bottom sublayer reduced notably while the finger like pores at the skin layer become narrower gradually as compared to that of 0.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loading. This is because the doping solution can be further thickened with the increment in the additive loading from 1 wt% to 2.5 wt%, leading to lower diffusion coefficient and even higher de-mixing rate between the solvent and non-solvent [6]. As a result, the membrane pore size reduced with a higher additive loading. The membranes with 1.5 and 2.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loading as presented in Fig. 4(d) and 4(e) show densely packed and narrower finger-shaped structure. In addition, the inhomogeneous mixing of the CD/SiO2 powder in the highly viscous casting solution might lead to the agglomeration of the CD/SiO2 additives in the membrane pores [33].

Fig. 5 demonstrates the plane view of the membranes with different loadings of CD/SiO2 nanocomposites at a magnification of ×6k. From the SEM images presented in Fig. 5 (a) to (e), the porous structure is clearly visible in all the membranes with uniform distribution of pores, signifying the rapid mass transfer between NMP and water during the precipitation process. As we can see, pore size for all the membranes was observed in the approximate diameter range of 3−6 μm. The observation was in accordance with the membrane pore size shown in Table 3. Furthermore, it can be noticed that the pore structure of the membrane with 2.5 wt% of CD/SiO2 become less obvious. This might be due to the high concentration of additives in the polymer solution which resulted in irregular positioning of nanoparticles, therefore forming a dense selective layer on top of the membrane surface [12].

The elemental distribution of each element on the membrane is presented in Fig. 6 . The EDX analysis shows that the Si, O, C and S elements displayed a homogeneous and evenly distribution across the membrane, indicating the consistency in the membrane characteristics. As a comparison, the elemental composition of each element on the pure PSf membrane is presented in Fig S4. The PSf membrane was mainly composed of carbon (83.8 at%), oxygen (11.08 at%) and sulphur (5.12 at%) elements. Moreover, silica element was absent in the pure PSf membrane, signifying that no additive was embedded within the naked PSf membrane.

As discussed previously in Section 3.2.1, the incorporation of CD/SiO2 additives into the membrane would increase the pore formation and pore size of the membranes. Table 2 shows the calculated porosity and pore size for all membranes. The pure PSf membrane recorded the lowest porosity value of 83.65 %, and the porosity values were raised gradually with the increment of the CD/SiO2 loading. As discussed previously, the membrane porosity that was associated with the pore formation was observed to increase with the rise in the additive loading.

Overall, all membranes fabricated were proven to be UF membranes as the pore size of the membranes lie between the standard UF membrane pore size range of 1–10 μm. Similar to membrane porosity, the pore size values increased when the CD/SiO2 additive loading was increased up to 1.0 wt%. Subsequently, the pore size demonstrated a declining trend for the 1.5 and 2.5 wt% loading. This might be caused by the agglomeration of the excessive amount of CD/SiO2 additive particles that caused the casting solution to be more viscous, which in turns slow down the exchange rate between the solvent and non-solvent during the phase inversion process [5]. The delayed mass transfer hindered the formation of large pores, thus resulted in smaller membrane pore size despite having high porosity.

The surface hydrophilicity of the membranes was determined by measuring the water contact angle. Low water contact angle where the contact angle is lower than 90 ° stipulates that the membrane has high water affinity, indicating that the membrane is more hydrophilic [34]. The hydrophilicity of the membrane can be associated with the membrane permeability by increasing the interaction between the water molecules and the membrane polymer, which in turns raises the membrane permeability [22].

Table 3 shows the water contact angle of all membranes. Pure PSf membrane shows the highest water contact angle due to the absence of the hydrophilic CD/SiO2 additives. When the hydrophilic CD/SiO2 loading was increased from 0 to 1.0 wt%, the water contact angle was observed to decline steadily, suggesting that the membranes with the addition of CD/SiO2 became more hydrophilic. As the CD/SiO2 loading increased further, the water contact angle increased slightly. This might be caused by the presence of excessive CD/SiO2 nanoparticles on the membrane which tend to form aggregates which increase the surface roughness of the polymer membrane. The aggregates that positioned irregularly on the surface reduced the interaction between the water molecule and the membrane polymer, hence resulted in higher contact angle. A similar observation has been reported in previous studies [35,36].

Fig. 7 illustrates the pure water permeability of the fabricated membranes with various CD/SiO2 loadings from 0 to 2.5 wt%. The trend signifies that the membrane permeability increased gradually until 1 wt% of CD/SiO2 loading and declined afterwards. This was because the porosity and pore size of the membrane increased when more CD/SiO2 additives were added to the membrane casting solution, hence favoring the pure water permeability as discussed in Section 3.2.1. In addition, the increment of CD/SiO2 loading increased the hydrophilicity of the membrane, attracting more water molecules towards the membrane forming hydroxyl bond with the CD/SiO2 additive. However, as the amount of CD/SiO2 loading reached 1.5 wt%, the agglomeration of additive particles reduced the exchange between solvent and non-solvent, which caused the membrane surface to be rougher with reduced pore size and water permeability [5].

Fig. 8 depicts the dye rejection of membranes at different CD/SiO2 loading. The rejection test was conducted on the fabricated membranes using 10 mg/L of tartrazine, which is a common synthetic yellow food dye. The main factors affecting the degree of rejection include the membrane pore size and the membrane surface charge. When the membrane pore size is smaller, more particles can be retained by the membrane. Apart from that, the degree of rejection could be altered depending on the surface charges present on both membrane surface and dye particle. As tartrazine is an anionic dye, membranes with negative surface charges will demonstrate excellent degree of repulsion [37].

The zeta potential of SiO2 and CD/SiO2 measured was -1.83 mV and -19.57 mV, respectively. Referring to previous study by Gai, et al. [17], the surface charge of pure CD nanoparticle shall be in negative. This is due to the presence of oxygen functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl on the CD, increasing the electronegativity of the composite. Furthermore, the SiO2 with negative charge further increased the electronegativity of the composite. Hence, it was expected that all the modified membranes exhibited repulsion force towards the tartazine dye membrane [38,39]. In another word, more tartrazine particles can be rejected by the membrane when the loading of the anionic CD/SiO2 additive was increased. The prediction was supported by the results shown in Fig. 8 where the rejection percentage was observed to have generally increased with the CD/SiO2 loading.

Among all membranes, the 2.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loaded membrane recorded the highest rejection percentage due to its highest CD/SiO2 loading, which contributed to the negative charge of the membrane to repel more dye particles, and relatively smaller pore size that helped in retaining the dye particles [40]. Meanwhile, the pure PSf membrane exhibited the lowest rejection percentage due to its weakest surface negative charge arose from the absence of the negative charge contributing additives despite having small pores.

It can be observed that all the UF composite membranes show moderate performance on the removal of tartrazine dye. This is because UF membrane is preferred for the removal of high-molecular-weight dyes [41]. Tartrazine dye, as a low molecular weight azo dyes (534.4 g/mol) would therefore exhibit a comparably low rejection rate as compared to that in NF membrane. In the work by Hidalgo et al., compounds with lower molecular weight such as methylene green (653 g/mol) and amaranth (604 g/mol) showed comparably low rejection rates of 25.78 % and 13.85 %, respectively [42].

In this study, UF membrane was modified with the incorporation of additive synthesized from biomass. After producing SiO2 and CD from rice husks, the CD nanoparticles were grafted onto SiO2 through a hydrothermal treatment to form CD/SiO2 composite as the membrane additive. The membrane additive was then characterized in terms of functional groups, surface morphology, and crystallinity and structural characteristics. The Si-O-C linkage detected on the CD/SiO2 composite through the FTIR analysis and morphology study via SEM indicated that CD was successfully grafted onto SiO2.

A pure PSf membrane and a set of CD/SiO2 MMMs at different dosages of 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.5 wt% were fabricated and characterized in terms of surface morphology, hydrophilicity, porosity and pore size. The results revealed that the membrane with 1.0 wt% CD/SiO2 loading exhibited long finger-like structure and the largest pore at the top layer. Meanwhile, the 2.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loaded membrane exhibited the highest pore formation and therefore demonstrated the highest porosity. On the other hand, the pure PSf membrane showed the lowest porosity and smallest pore size due to the absence of the additive. This had proven that the presence of the CD/SiO2 additive in the membrane had contributed in pore formation and pore enlargement. Apart from that, the consistency of the membrane characteristics was indicated after observing an even distribution of the Si, O, C and S elements on the CD/SiO2 incorporated membrane through the EDX mapping.

The membrane performance was then evaluated in terms of permeability and dye rejection. All the modified membranes exhibited higher water throughput and dye rejection. Among the membranes, the 1.0 wt% CD/SiO2 loaded membrane exhibited the highest pure water permeability. This was attributed by the larger pore size and higher surface hydrophilicity of the membranes as discussed earlier. In addition, the 2.5 wt% CD/SiO2 loaded membrane showed the highest dye rejection due to small pore size and higher negative charge. This study shows that CD synthesized from biomass could be utilized to produce membrane with high permeability and rejection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to acknowledge Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman (IPSR/RMC/UTARRF/2019-C1/C07) for the grants provided. Besides, the support from our research centre, CPAMR is greatly appreciated.
